title: Net official development assistance to medical research and basic health sectors
subtitle: Net [official development assistance](#dod:oda) to medical research and basic health sectors. This data is expressed in US dollars. It is adjusted for inflation but does not account for differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Net official development assistance to medical research and basic health sectors

Net official development assistance to medical research and basic health sectors. This data is expressed in US dollars. It is adjusted for inflation but does not account for differences in living costs between countries.

The choropleth map displays the geographic distribution of net official development assistance directed toward medical research and basic health sectors across the globe for the year 2023. Values are expressed in constant 2022 US dollars, utilizing a continuous color scale that ranges from pale yellow for the lowest amounts near zero to deep navy blue for the highest amounts exceeding 300 million dollars. The highest levels of assistance are concentrated in specific regions of Africa and South Asia. India and Pakistan appear in the darkest blue band, indicating they each received more than 300 million dollars. A second tier of high assistance, falling between 100 million and 300 million dollars, covers a broad swath of sub-Saharan Africa, including Nigeria, Ethiopia, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Tanzania, and Kenya, as well as Bangladesh in South Asia. Moving down the scale, countries such as Brazil, Colombia, Peru, Indonesia, the Philippines, and Egypt sit in the middle bands, receiving between 30 million and 100 million dollars. Lower levels of assistance, ranging from 10 million to 30 million dollars, are visible in nations like Mexico, Argentina, Morocco, and Thailand. The lightest green shades, representing values between zero and 10 million dollars, appear in parts of South America like Chile and Bolivia, as well as scattered nations in Central Asia and the Middle East. Large portions of the map, including the United States, Canada, Australia, Russia, and most of Western Europe, are hatched to indicate no data, reflecting that these are typically donor rather than recipient nations in this dataset. The visual pattern shows a distinct focus of financial support on populous nations in South Asia and sub-Saharan Africa, with assistance amounts decreasing as one moves toward Latin America and smaller island nations.
